prompt: During a pre-trip inspection of a passenger bus, emergency exits must be:

options.text: Checked to ensure they open/close properly and warning devices work as required
Taped shut for the entire route
Ignored if the bus looks clean
Removed to save weight

explanation: Emergency exits must be operational and properly identified for evacuation.

prompt: Passenger bus brake checks are critical because:

options.text: Buses do not use brakes
Stopping distance and passenger safety depend on properly functioning service and parking brakes
Only steering matters on buses
Brakes are optional under 40 mph

explanation: Air/hydraulic brake systems must meet inspection standards before carrying passengers.

prompt: If an aisle light or interior lighting required for safe passenger movement is out, you should:

options.text: Carry passengers anyway without concern
Ask passengers to use phone flashlights as the official fix
Address the defect per out-of-service / company rules before operating with passengers as required
Remove all lighting permanently

explanation: Lighting defects can be OOS items when they impair safe boarding/evacuation.

prompt: Fire extinguishers on buses must be:

options.text: Optional decorations
Stored locked away where no one can reach them in an emergency
Empty to reduce weight
Present, charged/sealed as required, and readily accessible

explanation: Emergency equipment must be ready for immediate use.

prompt: A cracked windshield in the driver’s critical vision area is:

options.text: A serious defect that may place the vehicle out of service
Always acceptable if stickers cover the crack
Only a problem for cars, not buses
Fixed by dimming the lights

explanation: Vision must be clear; OOS criteria address windshield damage in the sweep area.

distractorExplanations.c: Buses have the same safety need.

prompt: Wheelchair lift / securement systems, when equipped, should be inspected for:

options.text: Cosmetic paint only
Proper operation, damage, and required securement belts/devices
Whether they make noise as a pass/fail only
Ignoring them if unused that morning

explanation: Accessibility equipment must function for passengers who need it.

distractorExplanations.d: Inspect even if not immediately used.

prompt: Tire inspection on a coach should include:

options.text: Only tire brand logos
Only mud on sidewalls as acceptable damage
Tread depth, inflation, cuts/bulges, and dual matching as applicable
Never checking duals

explanation: Tire failures endanger everyone on board; check to standards.

prompt: Emergency reflectors / warning devices must be:

options.text: Left at the depot always
Replaced by verbal shouting only
Used only for decoration on the dash
On board and available to set out during stops/breakdowns as required

explanation: FMCSA requires emergency warning devices for commercial vehicles.

distractorExplanations.a: They must travel with the vehicle.

prompt: Passenger seats and stanchions should be checked for:

options.text: Secure mounting and hazardous damage that could injure riders
Softness only for marketing
Whether seats recline to beds illegally
Ignoring loose seats

explanation: Loose seats/handholds are injury hazards in sudden stops.

distractorExplanations.c: Illegal conversions are not the inspection goal.

prompt: If the low air pressure warning fails during an air-brake bus check, you should:

options.text: Disable the buzzer and proceed
Not operate until repaired—warning devices are critical safety systems
Assume gauges alone are always enough without required warnings
Add air by pumping forever while loaded on the route

explanation: Required warnings must work; do not carry passengers with critical brake defects.

prompt: Baggage doors and exterior compartments should be:

options.text: Left open for ventilation at highway speed
Tied with string instead of latches as best practice
Securely closed/latched so items cannot fall onto the roadway
Ignored if empty

explanation: Unsecured doors/baggage create road hazards and damage risk.

prompt: Mirrors on a passenger vehicle must be adjusted to:

options.text: Show only the dashboard
Be removed to reduce drag
Point only at the sky
Provide required views along the sides and to the rear for safe lane changes and backing

explanation: Proper mirror setup is essential for a long vehicle with limited rearward vision.

distractorExplanations.a: Dash view is useless for traffic.

prompt: A first-aid kit on a bus, when required by rules/company policy, should be:

options.text: Present and adequately stocked/accessible
Optional trash
Locked in an inaccessible trailer not on the bus
Used as a snack box

explanation: Emergency medical supplies support passenger care until help arrives.

prompt: Steering play beyond acceptable limits means:

options.text: Better handling on ice
The vehicle may be unsafe/out of service until corrected
A reason to drive faster
No concern on passenger carriers

explanation: Excessive free play impairs control—critical with human cargo.

prompt: During post-trip inspection of a bus, drivers often note:

options.text: Only radio station presets
Nothing, because post-trips are never useful
New defects, passenger damage, and items left behind that affect the next trip’s safety
Only the driver’s lunch preference

explanation: Post-trips catch defects early and protect the next passengers.

prompt: Standee passengers on buses, when allowed, must:

options.text: Remain behind the standee line and not obstruct the driver’s vision or controls
Stand on the dashboard
Block emergency exits for comfort
Sit in the aisle during curves intentionally

explanation: Standee rules protect vision, control access, and evacuation paths.

prompt: Carry-on baggage should be stowed so that it:

options.text: Fills the stairwell completely
Does not block aisles or exits and is secured against falling
Covers emergency door handles
Is piled on the driver’s lap

explanation: Unsecured bags become projectiles and block evacuation.

prompt: When assisting elderly or disabled passengers, a driver should:

options.text: Rush them while moving the bus
Refuse all accessibility equipment use when required
Allow adequate boarding time and use lifts/securement per training and rules
Secure wheelchairs with bungee cords not designed for the purpose

explanation: Courtesy plus compliant securement protects civil rights and safety.

distractorExplanations.a: Never move with unsecured boarders at risk.
distractorExplanations.b: Equipment must be used when needed.

prompt: The standee line exists to:

options.text: Mark where to store fuel
Indicate the smoking section
Show where luggage must be thrown
Keep passengers from entering the area that would block the driver’s view or access to controls

explanation: Forward of the standee line is restricted for safety/visibility.

distractorExplanations.b: Smoking rules differ and are often prohibited.

prompt: Before departing a stop, the driver should:

options.text: Ensure doors are closed, passengers are clear of doorways, and the path is safe
Pull away while passengers are still in the doorway
Close doors on limbs intentionally
Skip mirror checks

explanation: Door entrapment and falls are major bus hazards.

prompt: Emergency exit paths must remain:

options.text: Blocked by coolers for convenience
Clear of passengers and baggage
Used as permanent storage for tools
Roped off so they cannot open

explanation: Evacuation speed depends on clear exits.

prompt: When loading at a curb, a common safe practice is:

options.text: Stop in the travel lane far from the curb forcing street boarding when a bay exists
Discharge into active traffic without care
Position the bus close/parallel as appropriate so passengers do not step into traffic
Encourage j-walking across highways mid-block as policy

explanation: Minimize pedestrian exposure to vehicles.

prompt: Child passengers / school-type operations (when applicable to your service) require extra attention to:

options.text: Maximum speed in loading zones always
Ignoring flashers/stop arms where required
Letting children hang out windows while moving
Loading/unloading procedures, crossing rules, and accounting for riders

explanation: Young riders need structured loading safety—know your operation’s rules.

prompt: If a passenger is standing in front of an emergency exit door, you should:

options.text: Direct them to move so the exit remains usable
Allow them to block it for the whole trip
Lock the exit permanently
Stack luggage against it

explanation: Exits must remain available throughout the trip.

prompt: Proper wheelchair securement typically includes:

options.text: Asking the passenger to hold the handrail only with no tiedowns
Using the vehicle’s approved straps/tiedowns at designated points, brakes set as appropriate, and passenger restraint as required
Parking brakes on the chair only with no floor attachments
Facing the chair sideways unsecured in the aisle

explanation: Manufacturers and regs specify securement systems—follow them.

distractorExplanations.c: Chair brakes are not full securement.

prompt: Overcrowding beyond designed capacity is unsafe because:

options.text: It improves fuel economy always
It is required for passenger endorsements
It blocks aisles/exits, increases tip/weight issues, and slows evacuation
It reduces stopping distance

explanation: Respect capacity and local rules; never compromise exits.

prompt: Announce stops / provide information when needed so that:

options.text: Passengers jump up during hard braking surprises
No one knows the next stop ever
People gather in the stairwell while the bus is moving at speed as standard
Passengers can prepare to exit safely without sudden movement

explanation: Communication reduces falls from unexpected motion.

prompt: Animals on buses are generally limited to:

options.text: Service animals and other animals only as permitted by policy/law
Any aggressive unrestrained animal anytime
Livestock in the aisle unbound
Pets blocking exits without carriers when forbidden

explanation: Follow carrier policy and disability law for service animals.

distractorExplanations.c: Livestock rules differ—aisles must stay clear.

prompt: When passengers are boarding with hot beverages, a safety concern is:

options.text: Hot drinks improve emergency exits
Spills causing burns during acceleration/turns—encourage secure lids/seating
Spills lubricate the aisle usefully
Drivers should accelerate harder to cool cups

explanation: Scalds and slip hazards are common; smooth operation helps.

prompt: The driver’s responsibility for passenger management includes:

options.text: Ignoring fights that block the aisle
Participating in altercations physically as first response always
Reasonable rules enforcement for safety without unnecessary confrontation escalation
Allowing riding on exterior steps

explanation: Maintain order for safety; use company procedures and law enforcement when needed.

prompt: At railroad crossings, buses often must:

options.text: Stop, look, and listen as required for passenger vehicles—do not shift gears while crossing
Race across without stopping when lights are flashing red
Stop on the tracks to check maps
Follow a truck so closely you cannot see tracks

explanation: Passenger endorsements emphasize rail-crossing stops and clear tracks before proceeding.

distractorExplanations.d: Keep enough space to see and stop.

prompt: If a bus stalls on railroad tracks, the priority is:

options.text: Stay seated to save time
Evacuate passengers away from the tracks immediately and then contact authorities
Try to push the bus while passengers remain aboard next to an approaching train
Ignore warning lights

explanation: Life safety first—get people off and away at an angle toward the approaching train’s direction of travel as trained.

prompt: Smooth operation of a passenger bus matters because:

options.text: Jerky driving improves comfort
Passengers prefer being thrown forward
Sudden starts/stops injure standing passengers and cause falls
Smoothness only matters for freight

explanation: Accelerate, brake, and turn smoothly with human cargo.

distractorExplanations.d: Passengers need smoother rides than freight.

prompt: When backing a bus is necessary, best practice is:

options.text: Back fast without mirrors
Rely on horn only without looking
Back into active pedestrian streams without spotters when available
Use a helper when possible, check mirrors, and back slowly the shortest distance

explanation: Buses have large blind spots; minimize backing.

prompt: In an emergency evacuation of a bus, passengers should:

options.text: Use the nearest safe exit and move to a safe assembly area away from traffic/hazards
Stay inside for photos
Gather on the roadway centerline
Return for luggage first during fire

explanation: Evacuation priority is people, not property.

prompt: Hydroplaning risk increases with:

options.text: Brand-new deep tread making hydroplaning inevitable at 5 mph always
Speed and standing water; reduce speed in rain
Dry pavement only
Using headlights on dry days only as the cause

explanation: Slow down in rain; worn tires worsen hydroplaning.

distractorExplanations.a: Good tread helps; speed still matters.
distractorExplanations.d: Lights aid visibility, not hydroplaning cause.

prompt: If you must stop on a highway shoulder with passengers, you should:

options.text: Leave passengers standing on the travel lane side casually
Skip warning devices at night
Activate hazards, set out triangles as required, and keep passengers away from traffic when evacuating
Turn off all lights to 'hide'

explanation: Visibility and separation from traffic prevent secondary crashes.

prompt: Railroad crossing gates that are down mean:

options.text: Drive around gates onto the tracks
Squeeze under partially lowered gates
Follow another bus onto tracks when space is tight mid-crossing
Do not cross—wait until it is safe and gates rise / signals allow

explanation: Never drive around gates; ensure enough space to fully clear tracks.

distractorExplanations.c: Don’t commit without space to clear.

prompt: Passenger comfort on long grades includes managing:

options.text: Speed, brakes (avoid fade), and transmission use appropriate to the vehicle
Riding service brakes continuously without regard to heat
Neutral coasting down long grades as best practice for air brakes
Turning engine off downhill

explanation: Control speed early; brake fade endangers everyone aboard.

prompt: In fog, a bus driver should:

options.text: Speed up to exit fog faster always
Slow down, increase following distance, and use appropriate lighting
Follow the taillights ahead as closely as possible
Use high beams when they reflect back blinding you

explanation: Reduce speed to visibility; avoid clustering on leaders’ lights.

distractorExplanations.d: High beams can worsen glare in fog.

prompt: If a fight endangers safe operation, the driver should:

options.text: Continue at highway speed while fighting occurs in the aisle
Join the fight
Stop in a safe place if needed, protect safety, and call for assistance per policy
Lock emergency exits so no one leaves

explanation: You cannot drive safely amid violence—stop and get help.

prompt: Mirror use before lane changes on a bus is essential because:

options.text: Buses have no blind spots
Turn signals alone move other vehicles
Lane changes never matter for coaches
Blind spots are large and passengers/vehicles can be hidden along the sides

explanation: Signal, mirror, blind-spot check, then move gradually.

prompt: When approaching a drawbridge with a signal requiring a stop (as applicable), you should:

options.text: Stop and not cross until it is safe / signal allows
Cross while the bridge is opening
Ignore signals if late
Follow a car closely onto a rising span

explanation: Bridge and rail hazards demand full compliance with signals.

prompt: Emergency after an evacuation: headcount matters because:

options.text: Headcounts are only for hotels
You must account for passengers and report missing persons to responders
Once outside, accountability ends
Only count luggage

explanation: Accountability guides rescue efforts.

prompt: Engine fires require:

options.text: Pouring gasoline on the engine
Keeping everyone seated over the engine
Evacuating passengers, using extinguishers if safe/trained, and not opening the hood carelessly to feed oxygen
Driving at high speed to 'blow it out' always

explanation: Life safety and controlled extinguishment beat improvised tactics.

distractorExplanations.d: Speed doesn’t safely extinguish engine fires.

prompt: Fueling a bus with passengers aboard is generally:

options.text: Prohibited / unsafe practice—discharge passengers before fueling as required by rules/policy
Required for time savings
Allowed if you smoke while fueling
Encouraged with engines racing

explanation: Fire/explosion risk makes fueling with riders aboard unacceptable in standard CDL teaching.

distractorExplanations.b: Time does not override safety rules.

prompt: Driving with an open passenger door is:

options.text: Allowed at highway speeds for ventilation
Prohibited because passengers can fall out
Required in winter
A substitute for AC

explanation: Doors must be closed while moving (with limited operational exceptions taught carefully).

prompt: Talking on a hand-held phone while driving a CMV is:

options.text: Required for safety
Allowed whenever you feel bored
Generally prohibited under federal/state distracted driving rules for CDL drivers
Safer than watching the road

explanation: Hands-free restrictions vary, but handheld use is broadly banned for CMV drivers.

prompt: Alcohol possession/use rules for passenger CDL drivers are:

options.text: Looser than for freight drivers always
Allow drinking in the aisle with riders
Permit driving with open containers for passengers and driver alike casually
Strict—alcohol misuse endangers passengers and violates FMCSA alcohol rules

explanation: Passenger safety plus federal alcohol testing rules demand zero impairment.

distractorExplanations.a: Rules are not looser for passenger ops.
distractorExplanations.c: Open container policies are strict; drivers must not be impaired.

prompt: Towing or pushing a bus with riders aboard is generally:

options.text: Prohibited / highly unsafe
Standard practice every morning
Required by endorsement rules
Safe if done at highway speed

explanation: Mechanical recovery should not expose passengers to uncontrolled motion risks.

prompt: Leaving the driver’s seat without securing the bus (brake set, etc.) when passengers are aboard can be:

options.text: Always required every minute
Unsafe and often prohibited unless proper precautions are taken
Fine if the bus is in gear rolling
Encouraged during railroad crossings on the tracks

explanation: Prevent rollaways; know attendance/securement rules for your operation.

distractorExplanations.d: Never leave a bus on tracks.

prompt: Refusing to yield to emergency vehicles is:

options.text: Required to stay on schedule
Optional based on mood
Illegal and especially dangerous with a busload of passengers
Only for cars, not buses

explanation: Move right and stop when safe; don’t block intersections.

prompt: Carrying unauthorized hazardous materials on a passenger bus is:

options.text: Encouraged to make extra money
Allowed if unlabeled
Required for P endorsement practice
Prohibited / tightly restricted—passenger operations are not general hazmat freighters

explanation: P endorsement knowledge stresses limits on hazardous cargo with passengers.

distractorExplanations.c: Not a training requirement to haul forbidden hazmat.

prompt: Allowing passengers to ride outside or on steps while moving is:

options.text: Prohibited
Allowed below 20 mph
A fun tour feature
Required when the bus is full

explanation: Everyone must be inside the passenger compartment as designed.

distractorExplanations.d: Capacity rules don’t allow exterior riding.

prompt: Falsifying inspection or hours records is:

options.text: A best practice for dispatch
Illegal and can disqualify drivers
Required if late
Harmless paperwork art

explanation: Honesty in safety records protects lives and careers.

prompt: Excessive speeding in a passenger bus is prohibited because:

options.text: Buses stop shorter at higher speeds
Speed limits never apply to CDL holders
Stopping distance and crash severity rise dramatically with speed and load
Passengers cushion impacts usefully

explanation: Obey limits and conditions; human cargo raises stakes.

prompt: Driving while ill or fatigued to the point of unsafe operation is:

options.text: Heroic and required
Only a problem for nighttime runs
Cured by energy drinks alone as compliance
Prohibited—out-of-service illness/fatigue rules protect the public

explanation: Fitness for duty includes rest and health.

distractorExplanations.c: Stimulants aren’t a substitute for rest/compliance.

prompt: Blocking a railroad crossing with a bus because you entered without enough clearance is:

options.text: Extremely dangerous and must be avoided by ensuring space to clear fully before starting across
Acceptable if you honk
Required when late
Safe if gates are down behind you

explanation: Never start across unless you can fully clear the tracks.

prompt: Using radar detectors in a CMV where prohibited is:

options.text: Required equipment
Against FMCSA rules for CMVs in interstate commerce
A substitute for safe speed
Mandated by passenger endorsements

explanation: Radar detectors are banned in CMVs federally; drive the limit.

prompt: Discourteous or reckless maneuvers (e.g., passing on the shoulder with a loaded bus) are:

options.text: Professional driving standards
Required to make up time
Unsafe, often illegal, and inconsistent with passenger carrier responsibilities
Encouraged in CDL manuals

explanation: Professional passenger service demands defensive, lawful driving.
